actions.thought: As this is the first hand of the tournament, I want to establish a solid, disciplined baseline. Holding Jack-Five suited on the button is a marginal hand that, while playable in a heads-up or 3-handed scenario, is easily dominated. Since I have no reads on the tendencies of my opponents, I prefer to fold and conserve my stack to wait for a more advantageous situation, rather than risking chips in a potentially disadvantageous post-flop spot.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Four offsuit. The button has folded, leaving it between me and the big blind. Q4o is a very weak hand to play out of position. While it is a blind versus blind situation, this hand lacks playability and equity against a standard defending range. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips early in the tournament.

On the button in a three-handed game, my range should be wide, but T3s is still a very weak hand. While it has some flush potential, it lacks high card value and connectivity. Given the stack depths and the early stage of the tournament, I prefer folding this marginal hand rather than risking chips in a potentially difficult post-flop spot.

In a blind versus blind situation with pocket Threes, I prefer to raise to take the initiative and try to win the pot pre-flop. Small pocket pairs are difficult to play out of position post-flop without hitting a set, so picking up the blinds now is a good result.

The opponent, who is in the Small Blind, raised to 300. As the Big Blind, I already have 100 in the pot. Calling 200 to see a flop with Queen-Five suited is standard defensive play given the pot odds and position. Re-raising is unnecessary with this range, as Q5s is a marginal hand that plays well enough in a checked-down or called pot.

The board of 8s 2c 9c connects reasonably well with the Big Blind's pre-flop calling range, which includes 9x, 8x, straight draws, and flush draws. Our pocket threes have little showdown value and poor equity if we are called. Therefore, checking to control the pot size and evaluating the opponent's action is the most prudent play.

The flop is 8s-2c-9c. I hold Qd-5d, which provides no equity, no pair, and no draw. The opponent, acting as the pre-flop aggressor, checked to me. Since I have absolutely no showdown value or draw potential, betting here would be a pure bluff without much fold equity against a calling range. Checking back is the most prudent play to see a turn card for free and reassess.

The turn brings the 7s, adding a second flush draw and completing several straight draws on an already very connected and wet board. My pocket threes are extremely weak here, representing underpairs to three of the board cards. Since I am out of position with negligible showdown value and poor equity against the Big Blind's continuing range, checking is the safest and most logical play. If the opponent bets, I will comfortably fold.

The board shows 8s-2c-9c-7s. I hold Qd-5d, giving me a gutshot straight draw to the six. The opponent, acting first, has checked to me on the turn. Since I only have a gutshot draw and Q-high, betting here would be an unnecessary bluff against an unknown range that could easily contain a pair or better. Checking behind allows me to take a free card to potentially hit my straight or re-evaluate on the river, keeping the pot manageable.

The river brings the Ten of Clubs, completing a four-card straight on the board (7-8-9-T) and a possible club flush. My pocket threes have essentially zero showdown value here. Given the extremely wet and connected runout, attempting a bluff into a range that likely contains many piece of this board (pairs, straights, flushes) is a losing proposition. Checking with the intention to fold to any bet is the only sensible play.

The board shows 8s-2c-9c-7s-Tc. I hold Qd-5d, which is Queen-high with no pair or straight. My opponent checked the river, indicating they may have a marginal hand or are looking to trap. Betting as a bluff with Queen-high is unlikely to succeed against a calling range that likely contains at least a pair on this board. Checking back is the most prudent decision to take my hand to showdown and minimize potential losses.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with T5o. This is a very weak unsuited hand with poor equity and playability. The standard and most optimal play is to fold and wait for a better spot to steal the blinds or play a pot.

The button has folded, leaving a blind-versus-blind situation. With Ace-Six offsuit, I have a strong enough hand to contest the pot against the big blind. Raising to 300 is a standard move to apply pressure and attempt to take down the blinds immediately, or at least gain the initiative post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc7d and the Small Blind has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 600, giving me 3:1 pot odds. J7o is strong enough to defend in this spot, especially since I will have position on Flashlight for the remainder of the hand. Folding is too tight given the odds and positional advantage.

The flop of K-9-7 is relatively dry but connects well with a big blind's defending range, as they likely have plenty of hands containing a nine, seven, or King. As I hold Ace-high without a pair or a strong draw, a continuation bet here would be ineffective against a calling range that has significant equity against my hand. Checking allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn, while potentially getting to showdown cheaply if the board runs out cleanly.

I have middle pair (7s) with a Jack kicker. The board has a King, which is a significant card for Flashlight's pre-flop raising range. Flashlight checked, which could indicate a missed hand or a trap. Since my hand has decent showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for three streets of value, checking back is the best play to control the pot size and realize equity. I can re-evaluate on the turn depending on the card and Flashlight's action.

The board of 7-9-K-8 is extremely coordinated and connects well with a Big Blind's calling range, as many straights (6-5, T-6) and pairs are now possible. My Ace-high has no showdown value against a made hand, and betting would likely only be called by hands with equity or better, while folding out hands I already beat. Checking is the optimal defensive play to control the pot and re-evaluate on the river.

I have a pair of 7s on a board of 7c 9h Kd 8s. Flashlight has checked for the second time, which often indicates a weak hand or a marginal holding looking for a cheap showdown. My pair of 7s has moderate showdown value but is vulnerable to several cards. However, betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands and might force me to fold if check-raised. Checking back is the most prudent play to control the pot size and realize my equity on the river.

The board shows 7c-9h-Kd-8s-4h, which is quite connected, allowing for potential straights (T-6, 5-6). With Ace-high and no pair, I have essentially zero showdown value. Since the hand has been checked through on every street by both players, my opponent likely holds a marginal hand or a missed draw. Betting here would be a bluff that rarely succeeds, as any pair will call, and I have no reason to turn my hand into a bluff. Checking to reach a cheap showdown is the standard and most profitable play given the lack of strength shown throughout the hand.

I have a pair of 7s on a board that contains several overcards (9, K, 8). Flashlight raised pre-flop but has checked on the flop, turn, and river. This passive line usually indicates a weak range, such as ace-high or a missed draw. While my 7s have showdown value, betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands and could lead to a difficult decision if I am raised. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and win the pot against his air or lower pairs.

I am on the button with Ace-Six offsuit, which is a playable hand in a 3-handed game. Since no one has entered the pot yet, I will open the betting to build the pot and potentially win the blinds, taking the initiative in position.

I have Ace-King offsuit in the Small Blind, which is a premium hand in 3-handed play. The Button has opened with a standard raise to 250. To maximize value and gain initiative while playing out of position, I will 3-bet to 850. This sizing puts significant pressure on the Button's opening range and discourages the Big Blind from entering the pot lightly.

Facing a Button open and a strong 3-bet from the Small Blind, AJo is generally dominated by the 3-bettor's value range (AQ, AK, TT+). Playing AJo out of position in a 3-bet pot, or sandwiched if the Button calls, is unprofitable. Folding is the most standard and disciplined action here.

Flash has demonstrated a passive playing style in the first few hands, only calling and checking rather than taking the lead. Given this conservative history, their 3-bet to 850 represents significant strength, likely a range of high pocket pairs or big aces. My hand, Ace-Six offsuit, is easily dominated by that range. Since I am only at the beginning of the tournament, I prefer to conserve my stack and wait for a more favorable situation rather than calling a 3-bet with a marginal hand that will be difficult to play post-flop.

I am on the button with K2o. In a three-handed game, the button should open a wide range, but K2o is at the bottom of that range and lacks playability post-flop. Given the stack sizes and the early stage of the tournament, I will opt for a disciplined fold and wait for a stronger hand to engage with, especially since my opponents have shown a willingness to play back in previous hands.

With A3 suited in the small blind and the button folded, I have a strong hand for a blind vs blind encounter. Raising to 3 big blinds will often win the pot outright and gives me the initiative if the big blind calls, plus my hand plays well post-flop and can flop flush draws or top pair.

I am in the Big Blind with J5s, which is a defendable hand heads-up against a Small Blind open. I have favorable pot odds (200 to win 400) and will have positional advantage throughout the post-flop streets. Calling is the standard and most profitable play here given the context.

I completely missed this flop and have no draw on a relatively wet board (Ts 6c Qc) that hits the Big Blind's calling range very well. Being out of position, a continuation bet is unlikely to fold out better hands or draws, so checking with the intention of giving up is the most prudent play.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-high on a Ten-Six-Queen board. Pro checked the flop, which could indicate a missed continuation bet or a potential trap. Given that my hand has minimal showdown value and only backdoor spade equity, checking behind is the most prudent decision to maintain pot control and potentially see a turn card for free. Betting into the pre-flop aggressor on a board that hits their range well would be an unnecessary risk, likely resulting in being called or raised.

The Ace on the turn gives me top pair, albeit with a weak kicker. Since the flop checked through, my opponent is unlikely to have a monster and their range is wide, containing many draws (like clubs or broadway straight draws) and worse pairs (like a Ten or Six). Betting here allows me to extract value from those worse hands and draws, while also protecting my equity by denying free cards. A half-pot bet of 300 is a standard size that accomplishes both goals.

The opponent, who is the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 300 into a 600 pot on the turn after checking the flop. While the Ace on the turn significantly improves their likely holding, the bet size is relatively small, offering 3:1 pot odds. My hand, Js5s, has a gutshot straight draw (needing a King) and backdoor flush potential. Given the favorable pot odds and the implied odds if I hit my straight or flush, calling is the most rational play to see the river card.

The river brings the King of Clubs, completing many draws. The board now has three clubs and four to a broadway straight (T-Q-K-A), making any Jack a straight. My pair of Aces is now nothing more than a marginal bluff-catcher. Betting here has no value as worse hands will fold and better hands will call or raise. Checking is the only sensible play, and I will evaluate facing a bet, though folding is highly likely given the coordinated board.

I have completed a Broadway straight (10-J-Q-K-A) with my Jack. The board has three clubs, which introduces the possibility of a flush, but my straight is a very strong hand. Since the opponent checked the river after checking the turn, they are likely either weak or trapping with a flush. Betting for value is the standard approach to get paid by worse hands such as two-pair or sets, while keeping the bet size manageable in case they hold the flush.

The river completes the flush draw and puts four cards to a broadway straight on the board (T-Q-K-A). My opponent's river bet indicates they likely hit a piece of this highly coordinated board, such as a straight with any Jack, a flush, or at least two pair. My pair of Aces with a weak kicker is merely a bluff-catcher and beats almost none of their value betting range. Folding is the safest and most optimal play here.

We are on the Button in a 3-handed game with Queen-Jack offsuit. This is a solid starting hand that plays well in position, so a standard open raise to 2.5x the big blind is the right play to try to steal the blinds or take the initiative into the flop.

My hole cards, 5d and 3h, are extremely weak and do not connect well. Playing this hand out of position against a button open is mathematically negative expected value. Given the early stage of the tournament, I should conserve my stack and fold, waiting for a more advantageous situation.

While the pot odds are somewhat attractive (150 to call into a total pot of 400), J4o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a button raise. In a 3-handed game, while ranges are wider, J4o still sits well below the threshold of a profitable defend. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Q4 offsuit is a very weak starting hand with poor playability and low showdown value. Even though I am on the button, there is no strategic justification to play this hand in a standard 50/100 blind structure. The most disciplined and profitable play is to fold and wait for a stronger holding.

With Flashlight folding on the button, the action is on me in the small blind. King-Deuce offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against a competent opponent (Pro) in the big blind. While a steal attempt is possible, K2o lacks the post-flop playability to justify a raise or a call here. I will choose the low-variance path and fold to wait for a stronger opening.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Standard play is to raise to apply pressure to the blinds and build a pot with a strong range. Given the stack sizes and the blinds at 50/100, a raise to 3x the big blind (300) is a standard opening size that allows me to take control of the hand with positional advantage.

7d 5s is a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand. Facing a raise from the Button, it is an easy fold from the Small Blind to preserve chips and avoid playing out of position with a marginal holding.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit against a button raise. While Ace-Deuce is not a strong hand, I am receiving roughly 2.25-to-1 pot odds, which justifies a call to see a flop. Being out of position is a disadvantage, but the Ace blocker retains some equity and allows me to play post-flop with a wide enough range given the tournament stage.

I am in the big blind against a button raise. On a board of 4s-3d-7c, my Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (need a 5) is relatively weak but has decent showdown potential if checked down. Betting into the pre-flop aggressor out of position is generally poor strategy as I want to keep the pot controlled and realize my equity. Checking allows me to re-evaluate based on the opponent's action, while the gutshot gives me some outs if the turn brings a 5.

I have Ace-high on a very dry, low flop (4-3-7) after raising pre-flop from the button. This board is unlikely to have hit the Big Blind's calling range hard. As the aggressor with position, I will make a standard continuation bet of about 40% of the pot to take down the hand immediately or protect my equity against overcards.

The pot is currently 900, and I need to call 250. Although my immediate pot odds are slightly unfavorable for a gutshot straight draw (approximately 21.7% needed vs 8.5% equity), I have an Ace-high hand that maintains some showdown value, and my gutshot draw to the wheel gives me significant implied odds if I hit on the turn or river. As the Big Blind, calling is the most standard play to keep my range wide and see one more card, given the effective stack sizes.

I am in the big blind with Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5 for the wheel). The board is 4s-3d-7c-Ts. I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. Checking is the most standard and effective strategy here, as it allows me to control the pot size and see if the opponent provides a free card or to re-evaluate on the river. Betting into the aggressor with only Ace-high and a draw is ill-advised when I can realize my equity more cheaply by checking.

On the turn, the Ten of Spades is introduced to a relatively low board. I currently hold Ace-high (AJ). Flashlight called my flop continuation bet, which suggests they likely have a piece of the board (a pair of 7s, 4s, or 3s) or a draw. By checking back on the turn, I control the size of the pot and realize my equity. If I bet, I am primarily turning my hand into a bluff, and it is unlikely Flashlight will fold a pair on this board texture. Checking allows me to see the river for free, where I might improve to the best hand with an Ace or Jack, or potentially win at showdown against missed draws.

I have Ace-high with no pair, and my gutshot straight draw did not complete. Since the turn was checked through, there is no value in betting as I would only be called by better hands. Checking allows me to reach a showdown at the lowest possible cost, as I have some minimal showdown value against a complete air-bluff and no reason to bloat the pot with such a weak holding.

On the river, the board is 4-3-7-T-Q with no completed straights or flushes possible based on the rainbow flop. I hold Ace-Jack high. After my continuation bet on the flop was called by Flashlight and the turn went check-check, Flashlight has checked again on the river. This sequence suggests Flashlight likely has a marginal hand like a pair of 7s, 4s, or a weak pocket pair that they are looking to take to showdown. While Ace-high could potentially be the best hand if Flashlight was floating with a draw or King-high, it is more likely they have a small pair. However, since I checked the turn, a river bluff is less likely to succeed in folding out a pair, and I have sufficient showdown value against their total air to simply check back and see the result without risking more chips.
Q8o is a weak offsuit hand, not strong enough to open from the button even in 3-handed play. The optimal and standard action here is to fold to preserve chips for better spots.

The button has folded, leaving me in a heads-up situation against the big blind. While my hand (Ts 2c) is weak, the cost to complete the small blind is minimal, providing an opportunity to see a flop with a favorable price. I will call to complete the blind.

Flashlight completed the small blind after Pro folded on the button. I am in the big blind with Queen-five offsuit. Q5o is a marginal hand, and while I could raise to try and take the pot pre-flop, checking allows me to see a free flop and realize my equity without risking more chips. Given the stack sizes and the early stage of the tournament, keeping the pot small with weak hands is a sound strategy.

I have top pair (Tens) with a weak kicker on a paired board. Since this was a checked-around pre-flop scenario in a limped pot, my range is wide, but I currently hold the best hand against a standard big blind check. Checking is the optimal play to maintain pot control and see how my opponent reacts to the board texture, as betting into a paired board out of position can be risky with a marginal kicker.

I have Queen-high on a paired board of Td-8d-8s. Flashlight limped from the small blind and checked the flop. Since I am in the big blind and checked pre-flop, the pot is small. There is no reason to bluff here as my hand has very little equity and Flashlight could easily have a piece of this board or a pocket pair. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card and potentially improve or reach showdown cheaply with my Queen-high.

The turn card (9h) adds to the straight possibilities on the board. My Tens with a deuce kicker are extremely marginal and vulnerable to many holdings. Since we both checked the flop, I should continue with a check to maintain pot control and realize my showdown value rather than betting into a board where I am often dominated or against equity-rich draws.

Flashlight has checked again on the turn after a passive pre-flop and flop. My Queen-high has very little equity and no significant draws on this T-8-8-9 board. While Flashlight's checks suggest weakness, betting into a small 200-chip pot with complete air is unnecessary. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and potentially win at showdown if Flashlight also has a very weak hand.

The board has paired the eights three times, giving me a full house (Tens full of Eights) with my Ten in the hole. This is a very strong hand, essentially a 'near-nuts' scenario unless the opponent specifically holds an 8 for quads, which is unlikely given the checked-down nature of the hand. Since the pot is small and we have both shown weakness, I should attempt to extract some value on the river against a weaker Ten or a pair that might feel compelled to call a small bet.

On a board of Td-8d-8s-9h-8c, Flashlight has led out for 150 into a 200 pot after checking all previous streets. My hand is only Queen-high. While the three eights on the board make it possible for me to win if he is bluffing with a lower high card, his bet represents at least a pair or better. Even a missed draw or a hand like Ace-high or King-high would beat my Queen-five. Given the passive nature of the hand until now, there is no reason to believe my Queen-high is good here, and calling a 75% pot bet is not profitable.

I am holding 6d 4h, which is a trash hand with no playable value or equity. Given that I am on the button and the pot is small, there is no reason to invest chips into this hand. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Ten-Four offsuit is a very weak hand even in a blind-vs-blind situation. Against 'Pro' in the big blind, who has shown reasonable aggression in earlier hands, this hand has poor playability post-flop and is likely behind his defending range. Folding is the most disciplined play to avoid losing chips in a marginal spot.

I am on the button with Ace-Deuce suited, which is a strong hand in three-handed play. I have positional advantage and want to build the pot or take it down pre-flop. A standard opening raise to 3x the big blind is appropriate here.

With 6d5c (offsuit connectors) in the Small Blind against a Button raise, the hand is simply too weak to defend profitably. Being out of position and lacking strong equity or suitedness makes folding the best and most standard choice.

3-2 offsuit is among the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While I am getting 2:1 odds to call (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650), my equity is very poor, and playing out of position against the button makes it very difficult to realize any equity post-flop. It is mathematically and strategically correct to fold.
Jack-Six offsuit is a very weak starting hand, even from the button. There is no reason to get involved with such marginal cards. The most prudent and profitable long-term decision is to fold.

I am in the small blind with 7h-Th after the button has folded. The pot odds (50 to call into a pot of 150) make completing the blind a standard and profitable play. I do not have a strong enough hand to raise against the big blind, so calling to see a flop is the most effective strategy.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has completed, meaning I can check to see a free flop. While I could raise as a bluff, T6o is a weak hand to play for a larger pot if called. Checking is the standard and safest play here to realize my equity for free.

The board [3h, 9c, Js] is disconnected and I have no pair or significant draw with 7h-Th. As the small blind acting first, checking is the standard and most prudent play to control the pot size and minimize losses in this weak position.

I have Ten-Six offsuit on a board of Jack-Nine-Trey. I have no pair and no real draw. Since the pot is limped and Flashlight checked to me, checking back is the most sensible play to see a free turn card and avoid bloating the pot with a weak hand.

The turn card, 7c, has paired my 7, giving me a pair of 7s. While this improves my hand from the flop, it remains a weak, marginal holding on a board containing a Jack and a Nine. Since the opponent checked the flop, they may be playing passively with a marginal hand, a draw, or a slow-played monster. Checking allows me to keep the pot small and control the pace, as betting into a likely stronger or trapping range would be ill-advised with only bottom pair. I will check to see the opponent's reaction.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. The board is Jack-Nine-Trey-Seven. Flashlight (Small Blind) limped pre-flop and has checked both the flop and the turn. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8). Since the pot is small and my hand has very little showdown value but some drawing potential, checking back to see a free river card is the most prudent play. There is no need to bloat the pot or attempt a bluff when I can realize my equity for free.

The river card is an Ace, which significantly improves the Big Blind's potential range. My pair of 7s is very weak, effectively functioning as a bluff-catcher at best. Since the opponent checked both the flop and turn, they may have a marginal hand or a missed draw, but they are very likely to fold weak hands to a bet and call with anything that beats my 7s. Betting for value is not viable. Checking is the most prudent play to see the showdown as cheaply as possible.

I am in the Big Blind in a limped pot. The board is Jack-high with an Ace on the river. Flashlight (Small Blind) has checked every street, including the river. I have Ten-high, which has almost no showdown value. While the Ace on the river is a good card to represent with a bluff, the pot is very small (200 chips) and the risk of being called by a weak pair or a better high card isn't worth the reward. I will check and concede the pot if I am beaten.

6c-2c is a very weak hand with extremely poor playability and showdown value. Even in a 3-handed game where position is an advantage on the button, this hand does not meet the criteria for a profitable open or call. The best strategic move is to fold and preserve my stack for a more viable opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with Qd 8h and it has been folded to me. Q8o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in a blind-vs-blind situation to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take the pot down immediately. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

I am in the Big Blind with J4o, which is a very weak, unsuited hand. The Small Blind raised to 3 big blinds. Calling or 3-betting with such a poor starting hand from the big blind against an open-raise is highly unprofitable. Folding is the correct move.

I am on the button with Queen-Four offsuit. While 3-handed play allows for a wider opening range from the button, Q4o is significantly below the threshold for a profitable steal, especially with a chip lead that allows me to wait for better spots. Both opponents have shown they are willing to defend or fold reasonably, but this hand has poor playability post-flop if called.

I am in the Small Blind with 5d 3h, which is an extremely weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. Even in a blind versus blind situation, completing here is a negative expected value play over time. Folding is the best choice to preserve my stack.
On the button in a 3-handed game, K8o is strong enough to attempt a steal. I will open to 2.5x the big blind to pressure the blinds and potentially take down the pot uncontested.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit. This is a very weak hand, especially when playing out of position against a button raise. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop without the initiative, and 3-betting with such a marginal holding is not profitable. The best strategic move is to fold and conserve my stack for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind holding J5o. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. This is a 2.5x open. While the pot odds are somewhat attractive (150 to call into 400), J5o is a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop, especially out of position. Against a competent player, defending such a marginal hand usually leads to negative expected value in the long run. I will choose the disciplined fold here.

Being on the button in a 3-handed game, 9-10 offsuit is a playable hand for an opening raise. The table has been relatively passive, and raising from the button is a standard move to take control of the pot and potentially steal the blinds. A raise to 250 is consistent with the standard opening sizes observed at this table.

I am in the Small Blind with 3h 8d, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight opened with a raise to 250 from the Button. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here as I am out of position and have very little equity or playability with these cards.
J2 offsuit is a very weak hand, lacking high card strength, playability, or connectedness. Facing a raise from the Button, it is an easy fold from the Big Blind.

I am on the button with 6d 4d, a suited one-gapper. In a three-handed game, this is a reasonable hand to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot with position. I will raise to 300 to maintain my aggressive image and potentially win the blinds immediately.
I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak offsuit hand (8c4s). The Button has raised to 300. This is an easy fold.

9-5 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor equity and board coverage. While the price to call is discounted, defending the big blind with this hand against an active opponent like Flash is not profitable. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve stack for better opportunities.

With Jack-Nine offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game, the hand is marginal. While it has some steal potential, folding is a safe and standard play to avoid difficult post-flop spots with unsuited, gapped cards. I'll conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the small blind with a weak starting hand (5-6 offsuit). However, since the button folded, this is a blind-versus-blind situation. I have already posted 50 chips, and the pot is 150, meaning I only need to call 50 more to see a flop. Getting 2:1 pot odds, it is mathematically sound to call and see if I can connect with the board, rather than folding and surrendering the 50 chips immediately.

Flashlight has limped from the Small Blind. I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. While I have position for the rest of the hand, this hand is not strong enough to raise for value, and raising as a bluff is unnecessary when I can take a free flop. I will check and see the flop.

The board texture is 2h-3d-8d, and I hold 5c-6h, giving me a gutshot straight draw (requiring a 4). With no pair or strong draw, betting into my opponent at this stage is unlikely to yield a fold from a range that includes many 8s or better high cards. Checking is the most strategic play to control the pot size and potentially see a turn card for free, as I have limited equity against a calling range.

I have flopped middle pair (threes) on a low, somewhat coordinated board. Flashlight checked to me. While I have a pair, it is quite vulnerable to overcards and draws. However, checking back allows me to control the pot size and see the turn for free, which is a solid strategy with a marginal made hand in a limped pot. Betting here might force out hands I beat or induce a check-raise from better hands or strong draws.

I have an eight-out straight draw with 5-6 on a 2-3-7-8 board. The turn card has opened up straight possibilities for both 4s and 9s. Checking is the most strategic move to control the pot size and potentially see a free river card, given that I currently have limited showdown value and want to realize my equity.

In a limped pot, I have a pair of threes which is currently middle pair on a board of 8-3-2-7. Flashlight (SB) has checked both the flop and the turn, indicating a weak range. While my hand might be the best currently, it is marginal and doesn't warrant building a large pot. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and reach a showdown cheaply, or evaluate the river action. Betting doesn't achieve much as I'm unlikely to get value from worse hands other than draws, and I could get check-raised by a slow-played 8 or a straight draw that picked up equity.

The board has paired the seven, and my hand (5-6) plays the seven on the board as a pair with an 8, 6, 5 kicker. Since both opponents have checked down to this point, it is highly likely that we are all looking at similar low-value hands. Betting is unlikely to generate value as I would only be called by a hand that has me beat or ties me, and checking allows me to get to showdown with minimal risk.

The board has paired sevens on the river, making my hand two pair (sevens and threes). Flashlight has checked on every single street, indicating a very weak range or a missed draw. While I might have the best hand, there is little value in betting as I am unlikely to be called by many worse hands, and I would only be called or raised by hands that have me beat, such as an eight or a seven. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value in this small limped pot.

King-Ten suited is a strong starting hand, especially in a button position. A standard raise to 300 chips should help me take control of the pot and potentially steal the blinds or build a pot where I have a positional advantage post-flop.

I have been dealt pocket sevens (7d 7h) in the small blind. The button (Flashlight) has opened for a standard raise to 3x the big blind. Pocket sevens is a strong hand three-handed and is well ahead of a typical button opening range. While I could call to keep the pot smaller and set-mine, a 3-bet is more effective here to take control of the hand, potentially win the pot pre-flop, or isolate against the button if the big blind folds. Given the stack sizes, I will raise to 900 to put pressure on the opener and protect my equity.

I have an extremely weak hand, 8h4d (8-high offsuit), in the Big Blind. Facing an open raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, folding is the only correct move. There is no reason to invest any more chips.

I am in the button position with King-Ten suited. After my initial raise to 300, Flash 3-bet to 900. Folding is too tight given I have position and a strong suited broadway hand. 4-betting here would commit a significant portion of my stack and potentially bloat the pot with a hand that is better played cautiously. Calling 600 to see a flop is the most balanced approach, allowing me to utilize my positional advantage post-flop and evaluate the board texture.

I 3-bet pre-flop with 7d 7h and was called. The flop is 9h-Td-6h, which is a bit coordinated with two high cards over my pair and a heart flush draw possible. However, 77 still has decent equity and provides an open-ended straight draw (any 8). I am the aggressor and should maintain pressure. A continuation bet of about half the pot (1000) is appropriate here to charge draws and see where I stand against Flashlight's range, which includes many broadway cards and middle pairs.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a King kicker on a board of 9h-Td-6h. Flash's 1000 chip bet into a 1800 pot is a standard continuation bet given their pre-flop 3-bet aggression. While this is a somewhat wet board with potential flush and straight draws, my hand is strong enough to continue. Folding would be too passive given my equity, and raising risks inflating the pot against a range that may already have me beaten with an overpair or a set. Calling allows me to see the turn card and evaluate further with pot control.

The Queen of Clubs on the turn is an overcard that likely improves Flashlight's calling range (hands like QJ, KQ, or even QT). My pair of sevens is now very vulnerable, although I still hold an open-ended straight draw with any eight. Since Flashlight has been sticky, continuing to bet here is risky. Checking allows me to see the river for free if Flashlight checks back, or potentially call a reasonably sized bet to realize my straight equity. I am switching to pot-control mode.

After my pre-flop 3-bet call and the flop call, the board now features 9h-Td-6h-Qc. My middle pair of Tens has reasonable showdown value, but it is currently vulnerable to better hands like AQ, KQ, or QJ, which are firmly in the opponent's 3-betting range. By checking behind, I maintain pot control and avoid bloating the pot when I am likely behind. This also allows me to re-evaluate on the river and possibly catch a bluff if the opponent is betting a draw that missed, or keep the pot small if I am behind.

On this board of 9h-Td-6h-Qc-9s, my pocket sevens are quite weak. The board has paired nines, and there are three overcards (T, Q, and the 9s). While Flashlight checked back the turn, which could indicate a medium-strength hand like a Ten or a missed draw, there is very little value in betting here. My hand has some showdown value against missed heart draws or perhaps a lower pair, but I am unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call a bet. Checking is the most standard play to reach showdown cheaply or decide whether to call a small bluff if Flashlight bets.

I hold two pair (Tens and Nines) on a board of 9h-Td-6h-Qc-9s. While this is a decent hand, the board texture is wet and the opponent's 3-bet range pre-flop heavily includes Broadway cards like Queen-X hands (AQ, KQ, QJ) which would currently beat my pair of Tens. Since the opponent checked the river, they are either giving up with a missed draw or checking to induce a bet with a stronger hand (like a Queen or a full house with a 9). Betting is unlikely to be called by worse hands frequently enough to justify the risk of being check-raised or value-betting into a better hand. Checking behind is the most disciplined and profitable play to take the hand to showdown and minimize potential losses.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce offsuit (J2o). While being on the button in a 3-handed game allows for a wide opening range, J2o is significantly below the threshold for a profitable open, especially given the stack sizes of the blinds and their past willingness to defend or 3-bet. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

With 6s5s in the Small Blind and the Button having folded, I have a great suited connector to play heads-up against the Big Blind. Rather than just completing the blind, raising to 300 gives me the betting initiative, allows me to win the pot immediately if the Big Blind folds, and disguises my hand while maintaining strong post-flop playability if called.

Holding 7-3 offsuit, which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in No-Limit Hold'em, I have no reason to defend my big blind against a raise from the small blind. Being out of position with such a poor hand makes it difficult to realize equity post-flop, making a fold the mathematically correct and disciplined play.

Ace-Five suited is a strong starting hand on the Button. It has great playability post-flop, can make the nut flush, and blocks some strong Aces. A standard raise of 2.5x is optimal to potentially steal the blinds or play a built pot in position.

I hold a strong pocket pair, 7c7h. The Pro is on the button and has been aggressive with opening raises throughout the tournament. Being in the small blind against the button, I want to take the initiative and potentially neutralize their positional advantage. A 3-bet to 850 is a standard sizing to build the pot and apply pressure, while also allowing me to better define their hand range if they choose to proceed.

I am in the big blind with Ten-Four offsuit, a very weak starting hand. Pro opened from the button and Flashlight 3-bet from the small blind. Being sandwiched between two aggressive actions with such a poor hand makes this an easy fold. There is no reason to commit more chips to this pot.

I opened with Ah5h on the Button, which is a great starting hand, but now face a 3-bet from the Small Blind. A5s is an excellent hand to call a 3-bet in position because of its tremendous post-flop playability. It can flop nut flush draws, wheel straight draws, and pairs with an overcard kicker. Calling here allows me to realize my equity well and use my positional advantage against the aggressive 3-bettor post-flop.

The flop texture of Ac-Qh-Qs is extremely unfavorable for my pocket pair of sevens. As the pre-flop aggressor, I am the first to act. Betting here is likely futile because any Ace or Queen in the opponent's range will call or raise, and I am only folding out hands that are already worse than mine (though there are few). Checking allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn depending on the opponent's action.

I flopped top pair with a weak kicker on a paired board in a 3-bet pot. The Small Blind's check could indicate they missed with hands like KK, JJ, or TT, or they could be trapping with a stronger Ace, a Queen, or a monster like AA/QQ. Betting here would likely fold out the hands I am currently beating while only getting action from hands that crush me (such as AK, AQ, or KQ). Checking back controls the size of the pot, avoids getting check-raised out of the hand, and allows me to evaluate the turn while keeping their weaker pairs and potential bluffs in their range.

The turn card 2c does not improve my pocket sevens, and the board remains heavily coordinated with an Ace and two Queens. Since the Pro checked the flop behind me, they likely do not have a premium Ace or Queen, but they could still easily have a middle pair or a weak Ace. Betting here would only fold out hands that are already losing to me or force a call from hands that have me crushed. Checking is the most conservative and strategic play to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the river.

After checking back the flop, the turn brings a 2c, which changes nothing. Flashlight checks again, likely capping his range to under-pairs like KK/JJ/TT, give-ups, or potentially a slow-played stronger Ace or Queen. Betting here doesn't accomplish much value-wise, as worse hands (like KK/JJ) are likely to fold, and I would primarily get called or raised by hands that dominate my weak kicker. Checking back ensures pot control, keeps his bluffing range intact for the river, and allows me to safely navigate to a cheap showdown with my top pair.

My hand (7c7h) is effectively a bluff-catcher on a board of Ac-Qh-Qs-2c-3c. Since the flop and turn were checked through, it is highly unlikely I can extract value by betting; any hand that calls me beats me (any Ace, any Queen, or pocket pairs 88-KK). Checking is the standard play to reach showdown cheaply and see if my pocket sevens are good against a potential bluff or missed draw from the opponent.

Flashlight 3-bet pre-flop and then checked down all three post-flop streets. His range heavily leans towards underpairs (KK, JJ, TT), missed high cards, or rarely a slow-played monster or backdoor flush. Since my top pair has a very weak kicker (5), a value bet here is extremely thin. Worse hands like Kings or Jacks are very likely to fold to any bet, while I risk getting called only by a better Ace (like AT/AJ/AK) or a trapped Queen/Flush. Therefore, checking behind to safely realize my showdown value and scoop the pot against his weaker holdings is the optimal play.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a strong starting hand. Given the stack depths and my position, opening with a raise is the standard and most profitable move to either pick up the blinds immediately or play the pot in position against the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h7d, a very weak starting hand. The button (Flashlight) has raised to 300. Calling out of position with such a weak hand is generally unprofitable, and raising is too risky against two opponents with healthy stacks. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 3s, which is an extremely weak hand. Facing a 3bb raise from the Button, there is no justification to call or 3-bet. Folding is the mathematically correct and optimal play.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of sevens. This is a strong hand in a three-handed game, and I should open the action with a raise to take control of the pot and leverage my position. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

Holding J6o in the Small Blind facing a Button open raise to 3 BB is a trivial fold. The hand is extremely weak, has no connectivity or suitedness, and playing it out of position against the pre-flop raiser would be throwing chips away. Folding is the only viable option.

Holding Jd-3c is a very weak hand with extremely poor playability and equity, especially out of position against a button raise. The pot odds do not justify calling with such a marginal holding. Folding is the disciplined play to conserve stack size for better opportunities.
KJo is a strong starting hand on the button in a 3-handed game. I will make a standard open raise to 2.5x (250) to build the pot in position and likely play heads-up or win the blinds outright.

I am in the Small Blind with a very weak suited hand (4s, 2s). Calling a raise of 250 out of position with such a weak holding is mathematically poor and lacks playability. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. Although the price to call is only 150 more to win a total pot of 400, T3o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially when out of position. I prefer to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation rather than defending with such a marginal hand.

6s 8c is a weak, offsuit hand with very little playability. Even from the button, there is no strategic reason to invest chips into the pot with this holding. I should fold and wait for a more favorable hand to play.

With Flashlight folding on the button, I am in a Blind vs Blind situation against Pro. 10d 6d is a suited one-gapper with decent playability. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and attempt to take the pot immediately. If called, I have a hand that can flop well.
Facing a 3x raise from the small blind, my hand (T4s) is simply too weak to defend with, despite having position post-flop. Folding is the safest and most standard play.

I am on the button with Ten-Seven offsuit. In a three-handed game, this is a marginal hand that sits at the very bottom of a standard opening range. Given that I have been quite active in recent hands (raising in hands 23 and 25), I decide to tighten up my image and fold this hand to avoid getting involved in a pot with a weak holding against two capable opponents with similar stack sizes.

Ten-Five suited is a weak starting hand. Even in a blind vs blind situation, it's generally best to fold hands with poor playability and lack of high-card strength from out of position, rather than trying to force a steal or limping.
JTo is a solid starting hand on the Button in a 3-handed game. I'm going to raise to 2.5x the big blind to try and steal the blinds or take the lead going into the flop with positional advantage.

Pocket eights are a strong hand in a 3-handed game, particularly when acting from the small blind. I want to build a pot, take initiative, and isolate the button (Pro) who opened the action. A 3-bet to 800 is a solid sizing to accomplish this, allowing me to define my range and either take down the pot pre-flop or play a high-equity hand in position.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s 7h. The Button (Pro) opened for 250, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) 3-bet to 800. While Flashlight has shown some willingness to play light in the past (like Hand #21), 6-7 offsuit is too weak to call a 3-bet, especially when I'll be out of position against a potentially strong range. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

JTo is a fine hand to open from the Button to steal the blinds, but it performs poorly against a 3-bet. The Small Blind's 3-bet range likely consists of stronger broadway cards (AJ, KQ, etc.) and premium pairs, which dominate our hand. We are out of position against standard 3-bet ranges, and calling here would be a leak. Therefore, folding is the disciplined and correct play.

I am dealt 2h-5c, which is a trash hand with very poor playability. Calling from the button with such a weak holding is negative expected value. Given the current tournament stage and position, the most disciplined and professional action is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight suited. Since the Button folded, it is a blind-vs-blind situation. Ten-Eight suited is a solid hand in this spot, possessing good post-flop playability and connectivity. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and attempt to win the blinds immediately or set up a favorable post-flop scenario.

I hold Ten-Eight suited in the Big Blind and am facing a 3x open from the Small Blind. In blind versus blind situations, the SB typically opens with a wide range. T8s is a highly playable one-gapper that has excellent straight and flush potential. Furthermore, I will have a positional advantage over my opponent throughout the rest of the hand. Calling the extra 200 chips offers good pot odds and allows me to realize my equity profitably post-flop.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and the flop came Queen-high (Qh-6d-4c). Although I have not connected with the board, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet. The Queen is more likely to be in my range as the raiser, and a bet here can often force a fold from hands like small pairs or various high-card hands that the Big Blind might have called with. I will bet 300 into the 600 pot to maintain the initiative.

I have Ten-high with a backdoor flush draw and no direct straight draws on a 4c 6d Qh board. Facing a half-pot c-bet from the Small Blind, my hand lacks the equity to continue profitably. Floating or raising here with just backdoor equity is unnecessary and unprofitable, so folding is the best decision.

I am on the button with King-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. I have 9,450 chips. The standard play here is to raise to attack the blinds and take control of the pot. A 3x raise is standard for this position.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h2s, which is an extremely weak hand. The Button raised to 300. Playing this trash hand out of position against a pre-flop raise is heavily -EV, so this is a standard and easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with KTo, which is a strong hand for defending against a button open. The pot odds (200 to call for a pot of 450) are favorable, and playing in position is not applicable here as I am out of position, but defending the blind with this range is standard poker theory. I will call to see the flop.

I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Flash) with top pair (tens) on a paired board (5-5-T). Checking is the standard play here to allow the aggressor to continue with their range. It helps control the pot size and allows me to evaluate the opponent's strength based on their response to a check.

I raised pre-flop from the button with King-Queen offsuit and was called by Flashlight in the big blind. The flop comes Five-Ten-Five, which is a dry, paired board. This board generally favors the pre-flop aggressor's range more than the caller's range. Flashlight has checked to me. I will place a continuation bet of approximately half the pot to represent a strong hand or a Ten, and to capitalize on the fold equity provided by the paired board texture. My overcards still have decent equity if called.

I have top pair with a good kicker (King) on a paired board (5-5-10). The opponent's bet is small, representing roughly 30% of the pot, which suggests a continuation bet or a test. Raising might fold out worse hands that the opponent is betting for value or bluffs, while calling keeps my range wide and allows me to navigate turn cards effectively while keeping the pot size manageable. Given the relatively dry board, top pair is a strong holding and I should proceed by calling the bet.

With top pair and a good kicker on a relatively dry, paired board, my hand remains strong. Since the action is on me and the previous betting round concluded without a current wager from the opponent, checking is the optimal strategy to control the pot size and induce bluffs from an aggressive opponent. This keeps the pot manageable while allowing me to evaluate the river.

I continuation bet the flop with overcards on a dry, paired board and was called. The turn is a Six, which is relatively blank. Flashlight has checked to me again. Since my opponent called the flop, they likely have a marginal made hand like a Ten or a mid-pocket pair. I will check back to control the pot and take a free card to try and hit a King or Queen on the river, as a second barrel is unlikely to fold out a Ten on this texture.

I have improved to a full house (Tens full of Fives) on the river, which is a very strong hand. I am first to act on the river. I need to bet for value to get paid by my opponent's potential weaker hands (trips, lower full houses, or overplayed pairs). A bet of 500 into a 1250 pot provides decent pot odds for the opponent to call with a wide range while maximizing my return.

On a board of 5d-Ts-5h-6c-Td, the board has double-paired, creating a final hand of two pair, Tens and Fives. My best five-card hand is 10-10-5-5-K. This loses to any hand containing a Ten (full house), a Five (full house), or an Ace (better kicker), but it beats any pocket pair from 22-99 and any other high-card hand (Qx, Jx, etc.). Flashlight's lead of 500 into a 1250 pot provides me with excellent pot odds (roughly 3.5:1). Given that I checked the turn, Flashlight could be betting a wide range of hands including medium pocket pairs or air. While there are several hands that beat me (Ax, Tx, 5x), the strength of a King kicker on this specific double-paired board, combined with the favorable odds, makes this a mandatory call.

With 6s 5s on the Button, I have a solid speculative hand with good playability post-flop. Raising here allows me to steal the blinds or take control of the hand with positional advantage if called. A standard open to 2.5x the big blind is appropriate.

Pocket tens are a premium hand that I should play aggressively to take the initiative against the Button's opening raise. By 3-betting to 850, I can isolate the Button, build value in the pot, and discourage the Big Blind from getting involved cheaply.

I am in the Big Blind holding Kd 8h. Pro opened from the button, and Flashlight 3-bet from the Small Blind. Cold-calling a 3-bet with a marginal offsuit hand like K8o is a losing play in the long run, as I will be out of position against at least one player and the hand does not flop well. Folding is the correct disciplined choice.

I opened 6s 5s on the button, which is a fine steal attempt, but the small blind 3-bet to 850. While I have position, 65s is too weak and speculative to profitably call a 3-bet of this size. The risk of being dominated or facing heavy aggression post-flop without a strong made hand is high. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with King-Nine offsuit. In a three-handed game, this is a reasonable hand to open with, especially from the button, to exert pressure on the blinds and potentially pick up the pot pre-flop. My stack is healthy, and I want to maintain an aggressive posture to control the table.

I am holding 8-3 offsuit in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand with poor playability and equity. Flashlight (Button) has raised to 250. Even though I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call, defending such a weak hand out of position against an active player is a losing strategy. It is best to fold and wait for a better spot.

A5 offsuit is a marginal hand to play out of position against a button raise. While it has some blocker value for a 3-bet bluff, folding is the most solid, low-variance play here to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with Seven-Deuce suited, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. While being on the button offers a positional advantage, the hand's raw equity and lack of connectivity make it a poor candidate for a steal or a call, especially against aggressive opponents like Flashlight. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term play here.

97o is a weak offsuit hand, and playing it out of position from the Small Blind against the Big Blind is generally unprofitable. Folding is the standard and safest play here to preserve my stack for better spots.

Holding 5-3 offsuit on the button, this is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. It lacks high card strength, suitedness, and meaningful straight potential. Attempting to steal the blinds with such a marginal holding is unprofitable in the long run, so the optimal play is simply to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the small blind with pocket sevens after the button folded. This is a strong hand heads-up against the big blind. Raising to 300 is the standard aggressive play to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or set up a continuation bet on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Four offsuit. The Small Blind has raised to 300 (a 3x open). While I would have position post-flop, Jack-Four is a very weak hand that does not play well and lacks sufficient equity to justify a call against a standard 3x raise. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of nines (9s, 9h). In a three-handed game, this is a strong starting hand that plays well post-flop. Raising is the standard move to take initiative, define my hand strength, and potentially isolate one of the blinds or win the pot immediately.

Flashlight raised to 300 from the button. I am in the small blind with King-Four offsuit. While ranges are wider in three-handed play, K4o is a weak hand to play out of position against an open raise, particularly with a competent player in the big blind still to act. Calling would often lead to difficult post-flop situations without the lead or position. Therefore, folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Q4s is too weak to defend against a 3x raise from the Button while out of position. The hand has poor playability post-flop as it typically makes weak pairs or dominated flushes, so folding is the safest and most standard play.

I am on the button with Jack-Five offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand, even in a three-handed game. While the button provides a positional advantage, opening with such a wide range against active opponents like Flashlight and Pro is likely to be unprofitable in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with AKo after the Button folded. This is a premium hand, so I should definitely open with a raise to build the pot and extract value from the Big Blind. A standard raise to 3 big blinds (300 chips) is appropriate here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. Pro (SB) has raised to 300, and Flash (Button) has folded. The pot odds are favorable for a call, and my hand is strong enough to defend from this position. Calling allows me to see a flop with a positional advantage and potentially outplay my opponent post-flop given our stack depths.

I have top pair with top kicker on a somewhat wet board (As Tc 7c). Having the Ace of clubs is a great bonus as it blocks the nut flush draw. I want to bet for value and protection against various straight and flush draws, as well as to extract value from worse Aces (like AQ, AJ) and Ten-X hands that might call. A half-pot bet of 300 is a standard sizing to accomplish this.

I have flopped top two pair (Aces and Sevens) on a relatively wet board (As-Tc-7c). Pro is leading out with a bet of 300 into a 600 pot. Raising now might force out hands that are currently bluffing or behind me, such as lower pairs or air. By calling, I maintain the size of the pot and allow Pro to continue betting with worse hands or their draws. Given our stack depth, I am comfortable playing this pot defensively until the turn, at which point I will re-evaluate based on the card texture and further betting.

The turn card is the 8s, making the board wetter by completing some straight draws (like J9) and introducing a spade flush draw. However, I still hold top pair, top kicker (AK) and block the nut club flush draw with the Ace of clubs. I need to bet for value and protection to charge the numerous draws (flush draws, straight draws) and extract value from worse Aces. A half-pot bet of 600 chips is appropriate to continue building the pot while denying free cards.

I hold top and bottom two pair (Aces and Sevens) on a board of As-Tc-7c-8s. While there are straight and flush draws present, my hand is quite strong and likely ahead of Pro's range, which includes top pairs, weak pairs, and semi-bluffs. Raising at this point risks isolating myself against sets or straight-completing hands, or forcing Pro to fold bluffs that I want to keep in the pot. Calling allows me to control the pot size, keep Pro's range wide, and evaluate the situation further on the river.

The river brings the Queen of clubs, completing the club flush draw and several straight draws. Although I hold the Ace of clubs to block the nut flush, my pair of Aces with a King kicker has been downgraded to a bluff-catcher on this extremely wet runout. Betting here would likely only get called by better hands, such as flushes, straights, or two-pair combos that outdraw me. Therefore, checking is the most prudent action to exercise pot control and try to get to a cheap showdown against busted draws or weaker pairs.

I have two pair, Aces and Sevens, on a board that contains both a completed flush and a straight (J-9 or K-J). The board is quite dangerous. By checking, I realize my showdown value and avoid the risk of a check-raise from Pro, which would be difficult to call given the potential for them to have made a straight or flush. Betting for value here is thin, as I am likely only called by better hands, and checking behind allows me to win against any missed draws or bluffs Pro might have taken to the river.

Pre-flop on the button with 9d 2d. Even though it's suited, this is an extremely weak hand with very little playability or showdown value. It is far outside any standard opening range. The best decision is to fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

I am in the Small Blind and the Button has folded. I have Ace-Five offsuit, which is a playable hand in a heads-up situation against the Big Blind. Calling the additional 50 chips to see a flop is a standard, low-risk move to maintain a manageable pot size while holding a decent Ace.

I am in the Big Blind with 7d 4d. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has just called the 50 difference to complete. With a suited connector-type hand like 74s, it's generally best to take the free flop and see if we hit a strong draw or two pair. Raising here doesn't accomplish much against a player who has shown they are willing to call and see flops frequently, so checking is the most efficient play to preserve my stack while maintaining pot control.

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a connected, semi-wet board (5-8-6). Since the board offers various straight draws, checking is a viable option to control the pot size and see a turn card cheaply. However, betting small (100 into 200) serves to extract value from weaker hands like a single 5 or a lower pair, while also forcing overcards to pay to see the turn. I will bet 100 to define the hand and protect my equity.

I have an open-ended straight draw with 7d 4d on a 5c 8s 6d board (needing a 4 or a 9 for a straight). Flashlight has bet 100 into a pot of 200, giving me 3:1 pot odds. With 8 outs to the straight (approximately 17% to hit on the turn) and some back-door flush potential, calling is the most mathematically sound decision. Raising is unnecessary in a limped pot where I want to realize my equity cheaply.

The board texture is somewhat connected with the 8-6-5 and now a Queen. My hand is weak, but I have the opportunity to see a free flop in position on later streets against the chip leader. I will check and see if I can connect with the board.

I am in the small blind with Jack-high on a board of King-high with two hearts. I have one heart, giving me a backdoor flush draw. With no pair and against a range that likely contains high cards in the Big Blind, betting is too risky. Checking is the standard play to potentially see a turn card for free or evaluate the next street.

I have 5h 3s on a board of 8h Kd 6h. This is a limped pot from the small blind, and I checked my option in the big blind. I have no pair and very little equity on this board. Flashlight has checked to me. Since I have no hand and it is a small, limped pot, there is no reason to bet or bluff here. I will check and take a free look at the turn.

On a turn board of 8h Kd 6h Js, my hand of Jh 2c has improved to a pair of Jacks with a flush draw. Since the flop went check-check, the pot is small, and checking here keeps the pot manageable. This allows me to see the river for free or evaluate the opponent's reaction if they decide to bet, maintaining pot control with a marginal holding.

I am in the Big Blind with 5h 3s in a limped pot. The board is 8h Kd 6h Js and Flashlight has checked twice. I have no hand and no draws. While I could attempt a bluff, there's little reason to build a pot with zero equity in a limped hand. Checking back to take a free look at the river is the most sensible play.

The board shows 8h-Kd-6h-Js-Th. I have a pair of Jacks (Jh-2c). Given that both the flop and the turn were checked through, it is highly likely that neither player has a strong king, or else they would have likely bet for value at some point. Betting here risks value-cutting myself against better hands (like any hand with a king, or potentially a straight with Q-9) and forces out worse hands (like 6x or 8x) that might call a smaller bet or check-behind. Checking is the most logical play to realize my showdown value in this pot-controlled environment.

I hold 5-high on a board of 8h-Kd-6h-Js-Th. This was a limped pot and my opponent has checked every street. While I have no showdown value, the pot is very small (200 chips). Bluffing here is unlikely to be profitable as any small pair or even Ace-high will likely call a small bet in a pot this size. I will check and concede the hand.

I am holding 3c 8s, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given that I am in the button position, there is no reason to play this hand, as it has poor equity against both opponents and will be difficult to navigate post-flop. Folding is the most profitable long-term strategy here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 3d 6c, which is a very weak hand. The player in the dealer position (Flashlight) has already folded. This leaves me against the Big Blind (Pro). Normally, with such a weak hand, I should consider folding or completing the small blind, but given the structure and the fact that I am in a 3-handed game, 3-6 offsuit is almost never a profitable hand to play even from the small blind. However, since the pot is small and it's only 50 to call, I could theoretically complete, but I'd be out of position for the rest of the hand against a professional player. Folding is the safest and most standard play with garbage cards like 3-6 offsuit.

I am on the button with 87o in a 3-handed game. Opening with a 3x raise (300 chips) is a standard play to pressure the blinds and take control of the pot. While 87o is a marginal hand, my position allows me to play post-flop more effectively if I am called. I've had success picking up the blinds with similar raises in previous hands.
J4o is a very weak hand and easily dominated. Facing a button raise while out of position in the small blind, the standard and most profitable play is to fold.

I am in the big blind with 6d 4d, a weak suited connector. While the pot odds are tempting, I am out of position against Flash who has shown aggression. Given my stack size and the marginal strength of my hand, folding is the most disciplined play to avoid playing a bloated pot out of position with a dominated range.

K2o is a very weak starting hand. Even from the button, it is not worth opening, especially with a 54bb stack where we need to be somewhat selective to maintain our stack. Folding is the standard and correct play.
I am in the small blind with 3-2 offsuit, which is a very weak hand with little potential. Given the position and hand strength, folding is the most disciplined and optimal play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Q4 offsuit. While this is a marginal hand, my position gives me an advantage to steal the blinds. With a healthy stack of 15,850, I can afford to play aggressively to accumulate more chips. I will open raise to 300 to test the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Ten offsuit (KTo). This is a strong starting hand in three-handed play, especially when facing a button raise from the chip leader (Flashlight). While I could consider a 3-bet to take the initiative, KTo plays well enough to call and see a flop. Calling also keeps the pot size manageable while I am out of position for the remainder of the hand. I will call the 250 additional chips to match Flashlight's raise to 300.

I am in the Big Blind with a weak suited three-gapper (7c4c). Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, I am out of position against two players. My hand lacks the equity and playability needed to justify a call, even getting 3.5:1 pot odds, especially since I am the shortest stack at the table. The most prudent action is to fold.

I have flopped top pair with a Ten kicker on a board of King-High, Eight, Nine. While this is a strong hand, the board is quite coordinated with both flush and straight draws possible. As the player out of position who called a pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the initial aggressor (Flashlight). This allows me to see how they react to the flop. If they bet, I have a very easy call or potentially a check-raise depending on the sizing, but leading out myself might cause them to fold their weaker holdings that they might have otherwise bluffed with.

The board texture is King-high, which is quite strong for my opponent's calling range. My Q-4 offsuit has completely missed the flop and offers almost no equity to improve. Checking back is the best approach to control the pot size and avoid putting more chips into a situation where I am likely behind. I have no reason to bluff here given the board hits the likely range of the Small Blind.

On the turn, the board has paired with a second nine. My top pair of Kings with a Ten kicker remains a strong hand, especially since Flashlight checked back on the flop, which often indicates they do not have a monster or are playing a marginal hand cautiously. By leading out now, I can extract value from weaker Kings, pocket pairs, or draws (like heart flushes or straight draws with JT/76) that might have taken a free card if I checked. A bet of 400 into a 700 pot (roughly 57% of the pot) is a standard sizing to build the pot while protecting my hand.

My opponent, Flash, has led out with a bet of 400 on the turn into a King-high board after checking the flop. My holding of Queen-high has no showdown value and essentially no equity against a betting range that likely includes a King, a Nine, or potentially a pair of Eights. There is no reason to continue with this weak hand, so folding is the correct strategic decision to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with King-Queen suited, which is a premium starting hand in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy at 9150. I am in the Small Blind with 96o, which is a very weak hand. Flash raised from the Button. Playing 96o out of position against a pre-flop raise is a losing play. The standard and optimal choice here is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Three offsuit. This is a very weak hand to defend against a Button open. It is better to preserve my stack and wait for a more suitable hand to contest, as this hand has very little post-flop playability.

K4 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop. Even on the button, it is not strong enough to attempt a steal, especially since both opponents in the blinds have larger stacks. Folding is the best decision to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

With King-Nine offsuit in the small blind and the button having folded, I have a strong opportunity to win the pot pre-flop or take the initiative into a heads-up battle against the big blind. Raising to 300 is a standard move to apply pressure and define my hand strength, giving me control of the pot.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. Flashlight, the chip leader, has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. I am holding 8h 5d, which is a very weak starting hand with little potential. Playing such a hand out of position or even on the button is generally unprofitable. I should conserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 73 offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has already folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Pro). Pro has a relatively short stack of 5150 compared to my 9150. I am on the button with 2c 3c. Queen-four offsuit is a very weak hand with low playability and negative expected value when played from out of position. Even though it only costs 50 more chips to complete the blind, the Big Blind has a positional advantage and a large stack. Folding preserves my stack for better spots.
T3o is a very weak starting hand, not suitable for opening from the Button. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (7-2 offsuit). J5 offsuit is a weak starting hand with poor potential for post-flop equity. Given my comfortable chip lead as the tournament leader, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with sub-optimal holdings. Folding is the most disciplined decision to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Pro). I have Ace-Four offsuit on the button. While it's not a premium hand, in three-handed play, any Ace on the button is a standard raising hand to put pressure on the blinds. My stack is healthy at 9150 chips. I'll raise to 3x the big blind to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop.

With QJs in the Small Blind against a Button open, we have a strong, playable hand. I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (5-8 offsuit). Faced with a 3-bet from the Small Blind (Pro) after an initial raise from the Button (Flash), continuing with this hand is highly negative expected value. Folding is the only sensible strategic decision to preserve my stack.

I opened with A4o from the button, which is a standard steal attempt. However, Pro in the Small Blind has responded with a 3-bet to 900. K8s is a solid hand to open from the Button. It has good stealing potential, blocks some strong Kx hands, and maintains reasonable equity and playability post-flop if called. I'll make a standard raise to 2.5x the big blind to apply pressure on the blinds.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd Tc. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250 and the Small Blind (Flashlight) has called. The pot is currently 600, and it costs me 150 more to call. On a low, somewhat connected board of 4d 3c 8c, I am holding Qd Tc, which gives me two overcards to the board. I flopped top pair with a strong King kicker. The board 4d-3c-8c is relatively coordinated and interacts well with ranges that include low cards, which the initial raiser (Pro) and the big blind (Flash) could easily hold. On the flop of 4d 3c 8c, I am holding Qd Tc, which gives me two overcards to the board. The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has bet 350 into a pot of 750. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has folded.
