Calling with pure air against a bet size that is nearly half the pot is generally a losing play in the long run, especially since I am out of position. Without a pair, a draw, or a significant equity advantage, folding is the most prudent decision to protect my stack for better spots.

5-6 offsuit is a weak hand with poor potential for winning showdowns against two opponents. While being on the button offers a positional advantage, the hand strength does not justify committing chips to the pot, especially with a solid chip lead to protect. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit. Flashlight has folded from the Button, leaving only the Big Blind (Pro) to act. Ace-Ten is a very strong hand in this short-handed scenario, especially since Pro's stack is relatively short (61 BB). I should raise here to take down the blinds or play a pot in position if he calls. A standard 3x raise is effective.

A7s is a very strong hand blind versus blind. Against a standard Small Blind open, a 3-bet will fold out many of his weaker steals and build a pot in position if he calls. I will 3-bet to 900, which is consistent with my previous sizing in similar situations.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit. I initially raised to 300, and Pro (Big Blind) has 3-bet to 900. Pro's stack is approximately 5300 (53 BB). While A10o is a solid hand, it faces significant strength from a 3-bet in this position. However, since the tournament is short-handed and Pro might be 3-betting light to exploit my steal attempt, I could consider calling. But looking at the stack sizes, a 4-bet or fold is often better to avoid playing a bloated pot out of position against a relatively short stack. Given Pro's aggressive 3-bet and the fact that A10o is easily dominated by hands like AJ+, AQ+, AK, or JJ+, calling feels weak and folding might be too tight. However, in this specific tournament context, I will call the 600 to see a flop and evaluate, as A10o has enough raw equity against a wide 3-betting range in a 3-way game.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace kicker (Top Pair Top Kicker) on a board of 10-8-5. This is a very strong hand in a 3-bet pot. Since I am out of position and called Pro's 3-bet pre-flop, I will follow standard protocol and check to the aggressor. This allows Pro to continue with a wide range of hands, including bluffs and weaker pairs, and protects my range. I intend to continue if a bet is made.

The 5h Td 8h flop connects quite well with the SB's 3-bet calling range, which likely includes many medium pocket pairs, suited connectors, and broadways with tens or eights. My As7s completely missed this flop and lacks any immediate draws. C-betting into this board as a pure bluff is risky, especially considering the stack-to-pot ratio and the likelihood of getting called by pairs or draws. Checking back takes the free card, controls the size of the pot, and allows me to potentially spike an Ace or evaluate the turn.

On the turn, a King of Hearts appears, which is an overcard to my pair of Tens. Since Pro was the pre-flop 3-bet aggressor and checked back on the flop, they might be pot-controlling a hand like JJ or QQ, or they might have hit the King with a hand like AK or KQ. By checking, I am exercising pot control and avoiding a situation where I bet into a superior hand. I will evaluate Pro's action if they decide to bet.

The King of Hearts on the turn completes many flush draws and hits a portion of Flash's pre-flop calling range, such as KQ or KJs. My As7s has no piece of this board, no hearts, and no draws. While Flash checking twice could indicate weakness or a pot-control line with a marginal hand, bluffing into a three-flush board without any redraws is very risky. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, take a free card, and see the river where I can evaluate whether my Ace-high has any showdown value or if a bluff becomes profitable.

The river is a blank, and I have second pair with the best possible kicker (Ace). Pro, the pre-flop 3-bet aggressor, has checked back on both the flop and the turn. This sequence often indicates either a hand with moderate showdown value (like JJ, QQ, or a medium pocket pair) or a total air hand that has given up. By checking on the river, I am looking to reach a cheap showdown. Betting doesn't make much sense here because it's unlikely a worse hand will call, and I don't want to risk being raised off my hand if Pro was trapping with a hand like AK or a slow-played set.

The river is a brick (4c). Flash has checked to me for a third time, indicating a hand with marginal showdown value like a weak pair (66, 77, 99, 8x), a missed draw (QJ, J9), or Ace-high (AQ, AJ). While betting might fold out better Ace-highs or weak pairs, our line (checking flop and turn) doesn't represent much strength, so a bluff could easily get called by a stubbornly played pair or a trapped King. Ace-seven high has some showdown value against busted draws, and preserving my stack of 5300 is more important than attempting a high-variance bluff in a 1800 pot. Checking behind is the safest and most standard play.

I am on the button with Qh Td, which is a strong enough hand to open for a raise. Standard play here is to raise to 3x the big blind to try and take down the pot immediately or play a controlled pot in position. My stack is healthy, and a raise of 300 puts pressure on the small blind's remaining stack.

I am in the Small Blind facing a 3x open raise from the Button. My hand, 4d 3c, is a very weak, disconnected offsuit holding. Playing this hand out of position against an aggressor is highly unprofitable, so the standard and optimal play is to fold.

Flash opened from the button to 300. I am in the big blind with K-8 offsuit. Getting 200 to call into a pot that will be 550, this is a standard defensive play. K-8o has sufficient equity against a typical button opening range to warrant a call to see a flop.

The flop of 9-7-6 is highly connected and hits the button opener's range extremely well. With K-8 offsuit, I have essentially no equity, no pair, and no meaningful draw. Checking is the standard play in the Big Blind to see if Flash makes a continuation bet, but I am prepared to fold to any significant aggression on this board.

I raised pre-flop with Queen-Ten offsuit and was called by Flashlight in the Big Blind. Since Flashlight checked, I will make a standard continuation bet of roughly half the pot to maintain aggression and potentially take the pot down immediately. If I am called, I still have outs to improve on the turn.

The flop of 9-7-6 is highly coordinated and hits the button's opening range significantly better than my Big Blind defense range. My hand, K-8 offsuit, has minimal equity here, only a weak gutshot straight draw with the 8. With a bet of 300 into a pot of 650, the pot odds are roughly 3:1, but the potential for realization of equity is low given how often the button will have connected with this board. Folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable play in this spot.

KJo is a strong starting hand 3-handed, especially when opening from the button. I'll make my standard pre-flop raise to 2.5x (250 chips) to try and steal the blinds or take the lead into the flop in position.

I am in the small blind with Td-7c, a weak, unsuited hand that performs poorly out of position. I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Five offsuit. The player on the Button (Pro) has raised to 250. With the Small Blind folding, it is 150 for me to call into a total pot of 400. My hand has good equity against a wide button opening range, and the pot odds are favorable. I will call to see the flop and defend my blind.

I have Ad 5s on a Qc 4s Th flop. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser. This board favors the raiser's range more than mine, and I have not connected with it at all, holding only Ace-high. I will check to see how the button reacts.

With King-Jack on a Queen-Ten-Four flop, I have an open-ended straight draw to the nuts along with two overcards. This is an excellent hand to c-bet as a semi-bluff. A half-pot bet puts pressure on marginal hands, allows me to take down the pot right here, and builds the pot in case I hit my straight.

I am holding Ace-high (Ad 5s) on a board of Qc 4s Th. This flop connects very well with the pre-flop raiser's range, likely containing many Queens, Tens, and straight draws. I checked the flop, and the Button (Pro) has placed a continuation bet of 250 into a pot of 550. Since I have no pair, no significant draw, and am out of position, there is little reason to continue. I am on the button with King-Ten offsuit in a 3-max game. This is a strong starting hand in this format. Opening the pot is the optimal strategy to leverage my stack advantage and potentially take down the blinds or play in position. A raise to 300 is a standard opening size.

I am in the Small Blind with 92o, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Flashlight (Button) has opened for 300. Even with decent pot odds, 92o is nearly unplayable post-flop, especially out of position against a deep stack. Folding is the only sensible play here to conserve chips for better opportunities.

KJs is a strong suited broadway hand that plays very well post-flop. I flopped a very strong draw: a king-high flush draw with two overcards (Kh Jh) on a 7h Tc 2h board. This allows them to continuation bet with their entire range, and gives me the option to check-raise or check-call based on their sizing and my plan for the hand. I hold top pair with a King kicker on a relatively dry board. Since I opened pre-flop and have the initiative, a continuation bet is the standard play here. Betting approximately half the pot (350) allows me to extract value from weaker pairs like an 8, 9, or a lower Ten, while also providing protection against potential heart draws or overcards that could improve the opponent's hand on the turn.

I have a very strong draw with a King-high flush draw and two overcards. Facing a slightly larger than half-pot c-bet from the pre-flop raiser, I have excellent equity and pot odds to continue. The turn card is the 8 of spades, adding a gutshot straight draw (any 9 gives me a Jack-high straight) to my King-high flush draw and two overcards. Checking allows me to see if he continues his aggression, at which point I can evaluate pot odds to call or potentially look for a check-raise depending on his sizing.

I hold top pair with a King kicker. The turn card, 8s, adds potential straight draw possibilities (such as 9-6 or J-9) and flush draws, but my hand remains strong against a wide range of weaker hands like middle pairs or draws that Pro might be checking. By checking behind on the turn, I risk giving a free card to those drawing hands. A bet of 700 into the 1350 pot represents a standard value bet size that extracts chips from weaker made hands like a seven or a weaker ten, while forcing drawing hands to pay to see the river.

I have a King-high flush draw, a gutshot straight draw, and two overcards. Facing a half-pot bet of 700 into a pot of 1350, I am getting almost 3:1 pot odds (needing about 25% equity). With 9 flush outs and 3 additional straight outs, I have 12 outs to a very strong hand, giving me around 26% direct equity. This immediately justifies a call even before factoring in implied odds or the potential value of hitting a King or Jack. Shoving as a semi-bluff is an option, but calling to realize my equity without risking my entire stack out of position against potentially stronger made hands or the nut flush draw is the most optimal and safest play.

I made the second nut flush on the river with my King-high flush. The board is highly connected with a four-liner to a straight (7-8-9-T), meaning my opponent could easily have hit a straight (any Jack or Six) or hold a worse flush. Checking here is the optimal line: it allows Flashlight to bluff their missed draws or value bet worse made hands like straights or weaker flushes, setting me up perfectly for a check-raise all-in. Leading out might fold out their bluffs and weaker marginal holdings that they would otherwise bet.

The river board [7h, Tc, 2h, 8s, 9h] is extremely coordinated, completing multiple straight possibilities (any Jack or any 6). My pair of Tens is quite vulnerable given the action, as Pro has called bets on both the flop and turn, suggesting a solid holding or a strong draw that may have hit. Betting into a checked river here is likely to be called only by hands that beat me (straights, two-pairs, or better Tens). Checking behind is the most prudent path to showdown, minimizing potential losses if Pro is trapping or hit a straight.

I am on the button with King-Seven suited (Ks 7s), which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. Opening from the button allows me to put pressure on the blinds and ensures I will have position for the remainder of the hand. A standard 3x raise to 300 is consistent with my established betting patterns and balances my range.
J2s is a very weak and disconnected hand. Playing it out of position from the small blind against a button raise is a long-term losing strategy. Folding is the standard and optimal play here.

Ten-seven offsuit is a weak, uncoordinated hand. Defending the big blind with this holding against a raise from the button puts me in a disadvantageous position post-flop, where I will likely face difficult decisions without a strong made hand or solid draw. The pot odds do not justify calling with such a marginal range, and folding is the most disciplined and conservative approach to preserving my stack.

I am in the Big Blind holding 3d 7s, which is an extremely weak hand. Facing a raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, this is a clear fold. I need to preserve my stack for a much better starting hand.

Since I was the pre-flop aggressor with a 3-bet, leading out for value is the standard and most effective play. I want to build the pot and charge any potential draws (like diamond flushes or a straight draw with JT). A bet of approximately 50% of the pot is appropriate to keep worse hands calling while protecting my equity.

The flop is extremely unfavorable for my pocket threes. The board (K-Q-5) contains two high cards, which strongly hit the 3-betting range of the small blind, Flash. With no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw, I have effectively zero equity in this hand. Continuing would be mathematically incorrect. I will fold and save my stack for a more profitable situation.
I have pocket Tens on the button, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. I'll open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand.
T7o is a very weak hand, especially out of position in the small blind against a button raise. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Big Blind with a weak offsuit hand (8-6 offsuit) and I am out of position against an aggressive button raiser. While the pot odds are tempting, calling with such a marginal hand is generally not profitable in the long run. I will fold to preserve my stack of 10,750 and look for a more advantageous situation.

With 84 suited on the button in a 3-handed game, the hand is far too weak and disconnected to justify opening, even as a steal. A simple fold is the most profitable decision here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand, 3-2 suited. Given the low potential of this hand and the aggressive nature of the big blind player, it is not worth investing more chips to see a flop. Folding is the most disciplined and stack-efficient play in this situation.

Ace-Eight suited is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game, particularly from the button. Raising to 300 is the standard opening size to either take down the blinds immediately or play in position with a lead, consistent with my established strategy.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button. This is a standard opening range for a Button. Queen-Ten is strong enough to defend against a wide Button range, though I will be out of position. Calling 250 more to win a total pot of 450 provides decent pot odds. I will call and see a flop, while being mindful that Pro in the Big Blind has a relatively short stack and might consider a squeeze play.

I am in the Big Blind with T5o, facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind. T5o is a very weak hand and playing it out of position against two opponents is unprofitable. The standard action is to fold.

I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor on a dry board where I only hold overcards. Standard play here is to check to the button and see how they react. Leading out doesn't represent much strength given the pre-flop action, so I will check and evaluate based on Flashlight's betting size and frequency.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and have the positional advantage. The board is relatively dry (5c-3s-9s). Although I only have Ace-high, I hold the Ace of clubs which provides a backdoor flush draw. Checking is an option, but betting small allows me to continue my aggression and potentially take down the pot or gather information on Flash's range, as they often check-call with draws or medium-strength pairs. A c-bet of 350 (half-pot) is appropriate here.

On this dry flop of 5c-3s-9s, I have two overcards to the board (Queen and Ten). Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor on the button, has made a continuation bet of 350 into a pot of 700. This is a standard half-pot sizing that can be done with a wide variety of hands including pocket pairs, air, or strong nines. Given the pot odds of 3:1 and the fact that I have two overcards which could potentially be the best hand if I hit a Queen or Ten on the turn, I have a clear call to see one more street. Folding would be too tight against a likely wide button c-betting range.

The turn brings a second Trey, pairing the bottom of the board. This card is generally neutral for both ranges, although I'm still just holding overcards (Queen and Ten). Given that I called the flop bet out of position, the standard play is to check again to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading here wouldn't make much sense as I haven't improved, and I want to see if Flashlight continues their aggression or checks back for pot control. I will check and evaluate based on their sizing if they decide to bet again.

I am in position on the button with Ace-high and a flush draw. The board is relatively dry, paired on the turn with the three of hearts. My opponent has already check-called the flop, suggesting they have a piece of the board, a draw, or are trapping. Betting again into a player who called the flop on a paired board is unlikely to generate enough folds to justify a semi-bluff. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, take a free card to potentially hit my flush, and re-evaluate on the river, while maintaining some showdown value with Ace-high.

The river brings an Ace, which is a significant card. Since I called the flop and checked the turn, my range looks like it could contain some medium-strength hands or missed draws. Flashlight checked back on the turn, suggesting they might not have a powerhouse but could be looking for a cheap showdown with a marginal hand or Ace-high. Queen-high has almost no showdown value here, but bluffing out of position on this board is risky as Flashlight could easily have an Ace or a nine they are willing to call with. I will check and see if Flashlight checks back for a showdown or attempts a bet.

I have improved to a pair of Aces on the river (board Ah-5c-3s-9s-3h, holding Ac-8c). Flash has checked to me, and given that they checked the turn as well, they likely hold a marginal hand or are trapping. Betting for value is thin, as it is unlikely Flash calls with worse hands that aren't already planning to check-fold anyway. Checking behind secures the showdown and prevents being check-raised, allowing me to take down the pot with my pair of Aces if it is the best hand.

On the button in a three-handed game, Q8o is a marginal opening hand. While many standard strategies suggest opening around 50% of hands from the button, Q8o is near the bottom of that range. Given Pro's relatively short stack (51.5 BB), there is a risk of a shove that I cannot profitably call. To maintain my chip lead and wait for a more advantageous spot, I will fold this mediocre hand.

With King-Four suited in the small blind and the button folding, this is a clear opportunity to attempt a steal. K4s is strong enough to play post-flop if called, but a raise to 300 puts pressure on the big blind to defend with marginal hands.

I have been dealt pocket Aces, the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Since Pro opened the betting from the small blind, a 3-bet (re-raise) is the standard and most profitable move to maximize value and take control of the pot. Raising to 900 is a strong sizing that puts pressure on Pro's range while building a large pot with my premium hand.

I attempted a blind steal with K4s, but Flashlight 3-bet from the big blind. Calling a 3-bet out of position with a marginal holding like K4s is unprofitable, especially considering it would cost a significant portion of my stack. Folding is the clear decision here to preserve chips for better opportunities.

64o is a very weak offsuit one-gapper with poor playability and low equity. As the short stack with 49 BBs, opening this hand from the button is too loose. The best decision is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

With the button having folded, I am in the small blind against the big blind. Holding King-Eight offsuit is a mediocre hand, but it has sufficient strength to see a flop cheaply. I will complete the bet to 100 to see the flop with good pot odds.

I am in the Big Blind with J9o. Flashlight (SB) completed the small blind, which makes the pot 200 chips. I have the option to check and see a free flop or raise. Given that the hand is relatively mediocre and I have a significant stack, checking to see a flop and realize my equity for free is the most prudent strategy. I don't want to build a large pot out of position with J9o against a completion, which often contains a wide range of hands including some traps or speculative hands that will call a raise.

I have flopped top two pair (Kings and Eights) on a rainbow board. This is a very strong hand. I will lead out with a bet of 100 into the 200 pot to extract value from any King, pair of Eights, or drawing hands that might want to see the turn.

On this Kh-7c-8s flop, I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing any Ten). Flashlight led for 100 into a 200 pot. With 3:1 pot odds and a disguised draw, calling is a standard play to realize my equity. My Jack overcard could also potentially be good if Flashlight is leading with a mid-pair or a smaller draw. Raising is unnecessary and would bloat the pot with a marginal hand, while folding is too tight given the price and the gutshot.

I have improved to a full house (Kings full of Eights) on the turn. This is an incredibly strong hand, almost certainly the best hand at the moment unless my opponent somehow holds exactly the case eight, which is unlikely given the action. I need to continue building the pot and extracting value from hands like a King, a pair of Sevens, or any other marginal holding that might be sticky. I will bet 200 into the 300 pot to maintain a reasonable size for future value.

I am in the Big Blind with J9o. On the turn, the board is Kh-7c-8s-8h. Flashlight (SB) has led for 100 on the flop and now 200 on the turn into a 400 pot. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten), but the board has now paired the Eight. My direct pot odds are 3:1 (calling 200 to win a total pot of 800), meaning I need 25% equity to make the call profitable. My 4-outer gutshot provides only about 8.7% equity. Even if I consider implied odds or the slim possibility that my Jack is the best hand, the price is not right to continue chasing a non-nut draw on a paired board against an opponent showing consistent aggression. Folding is the most disciplined and stack-preserving move here.

I hold pocket Aces (AcAs), which is the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Being on the button, I have the positional advantage. The pot is currently at 150. A standard raise to 300 will help to build the pot, potentially isolate opponents, and capitalize on the premium strength of my hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Five of Hearts. The Button (Flashlight) has opened for a 3x raise to 300. While the hand is suited, Ten-Five is quite weak to play out of position against a regular raiser, especially with a shorter-stacked Big Blind behind who might look to squeeze or defend. Given my current stack lead, there is no reason to defend such a marginal hand out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 3s, which is an extremely weak, disconnected, and unsuited hand. Facing a button raise from Flashlight, I have very poor equity and will be out of position post-flop. The standard and only reasonable play here is to fold.

I am on the button with a suited hand (Jh 4h). While the 4 is a weak kicker, in a three-handed game, raising from the button is standard to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. I have a healthy chip lead and can afford to play this hand in position.

I am in the Small Blind with a very weak suited hand (6c 3c) facing a 3x raise from the Button. Playing this hand out of position is highly unprofitable, especially with the Big Blind still to act. Folding is the correct choice here.

8d 2d is a very weak hand, even in the big blind with pot odds. Defending against a button open with such a low-equity hand would be unprofitable in the long run, and I prefer to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am dealt 63o on the Button. This is an extremely weak hand with no high card value, suitedness, or connectivity. Given my short stack relative to the opponents, I need to preserve chips for playable hands. The best decision is to fold.

With the button folding, I am in a heads-up situation against the big blind. Completing the small blind with a suited Queen-high hand is a standard play to see a cheap flop, as the pot odds are favorable. I will call the additional 50 chips to complete the 100 big blind amount.

I am in the big blind with 7h 2h. Flashlight has limped from the small blind. Since I am already in the hand and there is no further action to call, the best play is to check and see a free flop with my speculative suited hand.

The board of A-K-9 is very disconnected but heavily favors the Big Blind's range, as Ace and King high cards are common in a defending range. With Queen-high and no significant draws, I have very little equity. Betting into the Big Blind here is a pure bluff that is unlikely to force a fold often enough given the card texture. Checking allows me to see a turn card for free and keeps the pot small, which is the most disciplined approach with this weak holding.

On this Ace-King-high flop, I have nothing but a backdoor heart flush draw and very little showdown value with seven-high. Flashlight has checked to me. In a limped pot, I should check back to see a free turn card rather than bluffing into a board that likely hits a limping range consisting of many Ax and Kx hands. My goal is to realize what little equity I have for free.

The board texture (A-K-9-8 with three spades) is extremely dangerous. My hand (Qd-2d) has no pair and lacks a spade to compete with the potential flush draw on the board. With only Queen-high, I have almost zero equity against a range that defends the big blind. Since the opponent has checked both the flop and the turn, I have no reason to escalate the pot with a pure bluff. Checking is the most disciplined move to maintain a low-risk strategy, keeping the pot small while looking for a free showdown or preparing to fold if the opponent finally decides to bet.

The turn brings the Eight of Spades, putting three spades on the board. I have seven-high with no spades and no draws. Flashlight has checked for the second time on a board that contains an Ace and a King. In a limped pot, there is very little reason to bluff here as I have zero equity if called, and checking back allows me to potentially see a cheap showdown or a safe river card for free. I will check.

The board is Kh-9s-As-8s-8c. My hand is Qd-2d. We have checked down through three streets. My Queen-high has essentially zero showdown value, but the pot is very small (200), and my opponent has shown no aggression. Bluffing into a passive opponent who has checked down the entire board is unlikely to succeed, as they likely have a marginal hand that they are content to take to showdown. Checking is the standard play here to minimize losses in a pot I cannot win at showdown with my current hand.

I have seven-high on a board with an Ace, a King, and three spades. The pot is very small (200 chips) because it was limped pre-flop and checked down on every street. While I have almost no showdown value, there is little incentive to bluff into this board when Flashlight has checked three times; they might be planning to call a small bet with any weak pair or even a better high card like Queen-high. I will check and concede the pot.

I am on the button with an Ace-high hand (Ace-Trey). In a 3-handed game, this is a strong position to open the action. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard move to build the pot and take initiative, putting pressure on the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-5 offsuit, a very weak hand. Flashlight, on the button, has opened with a raise to 300. Playing this hand out of position against an active button is not profitable, especially with the Big Blind still to act and potentially looking for opportunities to shove with a shorter stack. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
T8o is a weak offsuit gapper. Facing a 3x button open, playing this hand out of position is unprofitable. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am on the button with a strong starting hand (KQo). In a three-handed game, this is well ahead of my opponents' average ranges. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure on the blinds.

With 64s in the small blind facing a 3x raise from the button, calling is generally unprofitable as we will be out of position with a marginal speculative hand. Our stack of 46 BBs isn't ideal for flatting or light 3-betting against the button without a stronger holding or better read. Folding is the standard and safest play.

I am in the Big Blind with a solid hand, King-Jack offsuit, defending against a button raise. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 450), this is an easy call. KhJs plays well against a wide button opening range, and I have plenty of chips to maneuver post-flop.

The board is 2s, Qc, 5c. I have Kh, Js, which are overcards with no draw. As the out-of-position player against the pre-flop aggressor, leading out is inefficient as it forces me to build a pot with a hand that currently has very little showdown value. Checking is the standard play to control the pot size and see how the button proceeds.
I have top pair with a strong kicker (King) on a board with a flush draw. Flashlight checked to me, so I will bet 350 to extract value from weaker pairs or draws and to protect my hand.

I am in the Big Blind with KhJs on a Q-high, disconnected board. While I have two overcards, they provide very little showdown value against an opponent's C-bet on a board that hits the button's opening range well. With no pair and only weak backdoor possibilities, calling a continuation bet of over 50% of the pot is mathematically and strategically unfavorable. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

On the button with Ace-Three offsuit. A3o is a reasonable hand to steal the blinds with in a 3-handed game. I'll open with a standard raise to 250 chips to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take down the pot right here. If called, I have an Ace which can make top pair.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Ten offsuit. This is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Since Pro opened on the button, calling is the standard play here to see a flop and keep the pot size manageable, given that I am out of position. Raising could be an option, but ATo is often better played as a call against an opener unless I have a specific read to 3-bet. I will call the 200 to see the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 5d. The Button (Pro) raised to 2.5bb and the Small Blind (Flashlight) called. I need to call 150 chips into a pot that will be 750 chips. This gives me pot odds of 4:1 (requiring 20% equity). 95s is a strong speculative hand that plays well in multi-way pots, especially when suited. Given the excellent price and the depth of the stacks (excluding Pro's shorter stack), calling is the most profitable play to see a flop.

The flop is 7d-5h-2c, which is a very dry board that does not hit my range (Ace-Ten offsuit) particularly well, nor does it likely hit the ranges of my opponents given they just called pre-flop. I am out of position. Betting here would be a pure bluff, and since both opponents are still in the hand, the likelihood of one of them having hit the board (a pair of 7s, 5s, or 2s, or a pocket pair) is non-negligible. A check-fold is the most prudent strategy here to preserve my stack for a better spot.

On a flop of 7d 5h 2c, I have middle pair with a 9 kicker and a backdoor diamond flush draw. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has checked. As the Big Blind, checking is the most prudent play here to see how the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) acts. My hand has some showdown value but isn't strong enough to lead out for value into two opponents. I will check and look to potentially call a reasonably sized bet depending on the action behind me.

I have Ace-high and a gutshot to a wheel straight on a relatively dry and disconnected board. Both opponents have checked to me. In a 3-way pot, a continuation bet of around half the pot is often enough to fold out missed overcards and weak hands, allowing me to take down the pot immediately. Even if called, I have outs to a straight or top pair.

The flop is extremely dry with 7d-5h-2c, and I have missed entirely with Ace-Ten high. Facing a bet from the button in a three-way pot, my hand lacks sufficient showdown value or equity to justify calling, especially being out of position. As previously assessed, a check-fold is the most prudent strategy to maintain a healthy stack and wait for a more favorable spot.

On this dry flop of 7-5-2, I have middle pair (fives) with a nine kicker and a backdoor diamond flush draw. The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has placed a standard continuation bet of 350 into a pot of 750 after the Small Blind folded. My pot odds are approximately 3.1:1, meaning I need about 24% equity to continue profitably. Middle pair is often the best hand here against a range that includes many overcards like AK, AQ, or AJ that missed the flop. Given the price and the potential to improve on the turn with a 9, a 5, or a diamond, I will call to see the next card.

I have improved on the turn with a diamond flush draw to go along with my middle pair of fives. I am out of position against Pro, who was the pre-flop aggressor and bet the flop. Checking here is the standard and most effective play to see how the aggressor acts. If Pro bets, I have significant equity to call and try to realize my flush or improve to two pair/trips. If Pro checks behind, I receive a free river card and keep the pot manageable.

The turn card (4d) completes my gutshot, giving me an A-2-3-4-5 straight. While combinations like 6-8 or 3-6 would make a higher straight, those hands are less likely, though certainly possible given the preflop and flop calls. I have a very strong hand and need to bet for value and protection, especially with two diamonds now on the board. A bet of around half the pot will charge flush draws and worse straight draws, while also extracting value from one-pair or two-pair hands.

On the turn, the board is 7d-5h-2c-4d, and I hold 9d-5d for middle pair (fives) and a flush draw. The 4d also creates gutshot opportunities for a straight (a 3 or 6 would complete a straight using the 2-4-5 or 4-5-7 on the board and my 5). I have approximately 9 diamond outs for a flush, plus additional outs for a straight (three 3s and three 6s that aren't diamonds), totaling 15 strong outs. With 15 outs to the river, I have roughly 32.6% equity. Pro has bet 800 into a pot of 1450, making the total pot 2250. I need to call 800, giving me pot odds of 800 / 3050, which is approximately 26.2%. Since my equity (32.6% plus some showdown value from the pair of fives) exceeds the pot odds required, calling is the mathematically correct and most profitable play.

The river 9s is a fantastic card for my hand, improving my pair of fives to two pair (Nines and Fives). The board is 7d-5h-2c-4d-9s. While a straight is possible (68 or 36), those hands are less likely in Pro's range given the pre-flop action and consistent aggression. Pro, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet every street. By checking here, I allow Pro to continue his aggressive line, potentially bluffing with missed high cards or overcards that turned into a flush draw on the turn, or value betting an overpair like AA, KK, or QQ. Given Pro's remaining stack of 3200 relative to the 3050 pot, a check-call or check-raise is a strong strategy to maximize value from a range that has shown strength throughout the hand.

The river brings a 9s, completing the board as 7-5-2-4-9. My Ad 3h gives me an A-2-3-4-5 straight. The only hands that beat me are exactly 6-3 or 8-6, which are highly unlikely to have called the flop and turn bets unless they were specifically suited with diamonds. The opponent's range heavily consists of sets (77, 55, 22, 44), two pairs, or a busted diamond flush draw. Since I have a very strong hand, checking back would miss significant value against these made hands. A moderate value bet sizing is ideal to extract chips from his bluff-catchers, two pairs, or sets, without risking my entire stack. I will bet 1200 chips.

On a board of 7d-5h-2c-4d-9s, I have improved to two pair (Nines and Fives). The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has bet on every street, including a 1200 bet into a 3050 pot on the river. My hand is significantly strong here, beating all overpairs (AA, KK, QQ, JJ, TT) and any single-pair hands like A7 or A9. While straights (68 or 36) are possible, they are less likely given the button-opening range and the continuous aggression on a dry flop. Since Pro has only 2000 chips remaining behind his 1200 bet, and the pot is already quite large, a raise to put him all-in is the best move to maximize value from his overpairs or top-pair hands which are likely to call given the pot odds.

My A-2-3-4-5 straight is the lowest possible straight on this 7-5-2-4-9 board. Facing a massive check-raise on the river from Flash, who check-called both the flop and the turn, I have to evaluate his range. His line perfectly matches a hand like 68, which would have flopped a double gutshot, hit the straight on the turn, and maintained a stronger straight on the river. He could also have 36 for a higher straight. I am at the absolute bottom of my straight range ('the idiot end') and realistically only beat a complete bluff. With my tournament life on the line and 2000 chips remaining (20 BBs), calling off my stack here is a negative expected value play. I will fold and preserve my chips for a better spot.

I am on the button with suited connectors (6s, 7s), which is a strong starting hand for position play. Given the blind structure (50/100) and my stack size, raising to 300 is the standard opening play to build a pot, represent strength, or potentially take down the blinds immediately. This is consistent with my overall strategy of maintaining aggression, especially with a playable hand in late position.

I am holding 4s3s in the Small Blind. Flashlight raised from the Button to 300. While I have a significant chip lead, 4s3s is a very weak hand to play out of position, especially with Pro in the Big Blind sitting on a short stack of 1900 chips. If Pro decides to jam his remaining stack, I would be forced to fold anyway. At this stage of the tournament (Hand 115 of 150), protecting my chip lead is more important than taking a marginal, negative-expected-value spot with a speculative low suited connector.
With T3o in the Big Blind facing a button raise to 3bb, this hand is simply too weak to defend, even with a short stack. Folding is the only viable option.

I am on the Button with 82o, which is a very weak hand. While I have the chip lead, there is no strategic advantage to playing such a poor hand from the Button, especially with a short-stacked opponent in the Small Blind who might be looking to jam. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

With 18.5 Big Blinds left, I am short-stacked and need to be selective about the hands I play, especially out of position in the Small Blind. K6o is a marginal, weak offsuit hand. Rather than limping and inviting a raise or committing chips with a marginal holding, I'll fold and look for a better spot to utilize my remaining stack.

With QJo on the button and 18.5 big blinds, this is a clear opportunity to steal the blinds or play a decent hand in position. An open raise to 2.5x (250 chips) is standard, leaving me with enough stack to fold to a 3-bet or maneuver post-flop.

Ten-six offsuit is a weak hand with very little playability, especially out of position in the small blind. Facing a raise from the button, there is no mathematical or strategic justification to call or defend, as it will be difficult to realize equity in this pot. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision.

I am the chip leader with 16,150 chips, and I am facing a button raise from Pro, who is the shortest stack with only 1,600 chips total (16 BBs). My hand, Ad Ts, is a premium starting hand in a 3-handed game. Since Flashlight has already folded, it's just me and Pro. Pro has 1,350 chips remaining behind their 250 raise. I want to put maximum pressure on the short stack. A 3-bet here either wins the pot immediately or commits Pro to a hand where I likely have an equity advantage. Raising to 750 (a 3-bet) makes sense to force Pro into a decision for nearly half their stack, and I will comfortably call a shove.

I opened QJo from the Button and faced a 3-bet from the Big Blind. QJo plays very poorly against a 3-betting range, being dominated by hands like AQ, KQ, and AJ, as well as overpairs. Calling leaves me with an awkward stack-to-pot ratio, and shoving is an overplay with this holding. Folding preserves my remaining 16 big blinds for a better spot.

With a very weak hand of 4-6 offsuit, there is no strategic value in playing this hand from the button, even for a cheap price. Folding is the standard, disciplined play to conserve my stack and wait for a more playable hand, especially given that 4-6 offsuit has very poor equity against almost any range.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. Flashlight has folded from the Button. The action is now on me, and Pro is in the Big Blind with a very short stack of 1,500 chips (15 BBs). While I am the chip leader, Ten-Six offsuit is a weak hand that does not play well post-flop, and raising into a short stack risks a shove that I would be forced to fold to. I will choose to fold and wait for a better opportunity to apply pressure or eliminate the short stack.

I am holding 2d 4d on the button. While it is suited, it is a very weak hand. The small blind (Pro) is short-stacked with only 16 BB, making them very likely to shove over a button open. Flashlight in the big blind is also active and has shown a willingness to defend. There is no need to risk chips with such a marginal hand while maintaining a significant chip lead.

With KQo in the small blind and only 16 big blinds left, we have a very strong hand to play blind vs blind. We can either open-shove or raise to a standard size. A raise to 300 puts pressure on the big blind while allowing them to call with worse hands or mistakenly shove with dominated ones. If we face a 3-bet, we are fully committed.

I am in the big blind with Ace of Spades and Nine of Spades. This is a strong hand heads-up against the small blind, especially given the stack dynamics. Pro has raised to 300, and my hand is well within my calling range here. Given the potential stack sizes and the strength of A9s, calling is the most balanced approach to see a flop and evaluate, rather than overcommitting immediately.

The 6c 9h Th board heavily favors the big blind's calling range, which contains many straight draws, pair+draws, and two pairs. My KQ high has a gutshot to the J and overcards, but betting into this highly coordinated board out of position with a short stack is risky. Checking allows me to realize my equity for free or evaluate the situation if my opponent bets.

I have middle pair with a strong Ace kicker on a relatively connected board. The opponent, acting out of position, checked to me. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity, as betting here might only get called by better hands or hands with significant draw potential. Checking is the most prudent play to maintain pot control and see a turn card for free.

The turn brings the Queen of hearts, giving me top pair along with the second-nut flush draw (King of hearts) and a gutshot to a King-high straight. The board is incredibly wet (6c 9h Th Qh). Since my opponent checked behind on the flop, their range is capped and likely consists of marginal made hands, smaller flush draws, or straight draws. Betting here is crucial to extract value and charge those draws. With 1350 behind and a pot of 600, a half-pot bet of 300 starts building the pot and sets up a manageable stack-to-pot ratio for a river shove if needed.

The board texture is somewhat draw-heavy, and my middle pair of 9s has reasonable showdown value, especially considering the opponent checked the flop. I hold a pair of 9s with an Ace kicker on a board of 6c-9h-Th-Qh-Qc. The opponent checked the river after betting the turn. Holding Ten of Clubs and Five of Spades is a weak, unsuited hand. Playing this out of position in the Small Blind against an open raise from the Button is mathematically disadvantageous. It is better to conserve my stack by folding and waiting for a more playable starting hand.

I am on the button with King-Nine offsuit. This is a reasonably strong hand to open with in a three-handed game to attempt to steal the blinds and put pressure on the opponents, particularly the short-stacked Pro. A raise to 300 is a standard opening size to take control of the hand.

I have an absolute monster with Jacks full of Tens. I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand, 9-6 offsuit. Defending against a button raise with such poor cards out of position is a losing play. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.
